oss-issue-deep-dive.name: OSS Issue Deep-Dive
oss-issue-deep-dive.title: OSS Issue Deep-Dive
oss-issue-deep-dive.subtitle: A living doc for one stuck issue in an open-source repo — ready to load into a Claude Code or Codex session and keep updating as work progresses.
oss-issue-deep-dive.scope: One GitHub issue in an external OSS repo and its orbiting archaeology (related issues, PRs, code, maintainer stances).
oss-issue-deep-dive.objectiveRole: Make one stuck open-source issue operational by preserving symptoms, source anchors, attempts, related issue orbit, and maintainer stances as a handoff surface.
oss-issue-deep-dive.templateObjective: Replace with the objective of the focal issue — the one sentence that captures what would be different after the fix.
oss-issue-deep-dive.templateSuccessCondition: A fresh agent (or human) can load this doc into a session and start engineering work on the issue without walking the archaeology again.

attempt-log.name: Attempt Log
attempt-log.description: A record of actions taken against a problem and what each one proved. Distinct from findings (observations) and decisions (chosen directions).
attempt-log.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card is one attempt with an outcome. Status is attempt-log-status. Use when capturing tried fixes, probes, and shipped workarounds — not to record decisions or generic findings.
notFor: positions in an ongoing debate (use maintainer-stance)

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat each card as an action with a result. Every attempt must name what was tried and what it proved; shipped attempts must link their shipping site.
inspect: Verify each attempt has a concrete outcome — probes that revealed nothing are noise.
Check the shipped_in URL resolves and the referenced code or patch still exists.
update: When an attempt is superseded by a newer one, mark it superseded rather than deleting.
Preserve rejected attempts — they tell the next reader what not to try.
avoid: Do not collapse attempts into observations or decisions.
Do not mix generic notes into attempt cards — supporting context goes in notes[].

aiActions.name: Propose supersession
aiActions.description: If a newer attempt productionised the same insight, suggest marking this card superseded and linking the newer shipping site.

aiActions.name: Find shipping commit
aiActions.description: Search the referenced repos for the commit that productionised this attempt; fill shipped_in if missing.

code-anchor.name: Code Anchor
code-anchor.description: Revision-pinned pointers into source code. Each card is one file-and-range with a short description of what the code does and why it matters for the focal issue. Diagnostic, not product-framing.
code-anchor.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card pins a file path, line range, and revision (commit SHA or tag). Use when a doc needs to point a reader into exact code locations, not describe product capabilities.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Each anchor pins source code at a revision. Without a revision, a code anchor is not trustworthy — if the file moves, the pointer stales silently.
keepDistinct: why it matters for the focal issue
inspect: Verify the revision is resolvable (tag or SHA).
When the doc is re-crystallized, check whether the pinned file/range still matches current main.
update: If the code has moved since the pin, update the revision or mark the anchor changed-since-issue.
avoid: Do not use code-anchor for product capabilities or feature-level descriptions.

code-anchor.icon: <path opacity='.28' d='M4 4h12v12H4z'/><path d='M7 8l-2 2 2 2M13 8l2 2-2 2' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><path d='M11 7l-2 6'/>

aiActions.name: Check revision drift
aiActions.description: Diff the pinned revision against current main. Propose updating the revision, flagging as changed-since-issue, or replacing the anchor.

aiActions.name: Propose replacement anchor
aiActions.description: If the code moved, suggest a replacement path + range with the same purpose.

investigation-findings.name: Investigation Findings
investigation-findings.description: A set of factual findings recovered from an investigation, review, or forensic pass.
investigation-findings.structuralContract: Two-column card grid of page-status items, usually ground-truth findings. Use when the section is a collection of observed findings rather than decisions or implementation tasks.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat findings as observed facts recovered from an investigation, distinct from decisions or planned work.
inspect: Check the underlying investigation evidence before adding or changing a finding.
update: Keep findings factual and separate from recommendations or decisions.
avoid: Do not turn findings into decisions, formal claims, or generic status items.

aiActions.name: Check still holding
aiActions.description: Re-verify the finding against current evidence. Propose status transitions (ground-truth / reference / deprecated) or flag as stale.

issue-orbit.name: Issue Orbit
issue-orbit.description: A graph of sibling issues and PRs that share root cause, symmetry, or adjacency with the focal issue. Not a list of findings — each card is metadata about another issue.
issue-orbit.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card names another issue/PR, its state, its relationship to the focal issue, and why it matters. Use when a doc needs to preserve the shape of nearby bugs in the same problem space.
notFor: observations about the focal issue (use symptom-observation)

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Each card describes a different issue and its relationship to the focal one. The relationship is required — a card without a clear relationship does not belong here.
keepDistinct: relationship to focal (same-root-cause, adjacent, symmetric, superseded, prior-art)
inspect: Verify the github_state is current — closed issues may have reopened, open issues may have gone stale.
Challenge the relationship classification — a weak relationship is usually no relationship.
update: When a sibling issue is closed by a PR, link it via ticketIds or notes and update status.
avoid: Do not include issues with only topical overlap — the relationship must be structural.

issue-orbit.icon: <circle cx='10' cy='10' r='2'/><circle cx='10' cy='10' r='6' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='.4'/><circle cx='16' cy='10' r='1.5'/><circle cx='6' cy='14' r='1.5'/><circle cx='6' cy='6' r='1.5'/>

aiActions.name: Refresh GitHub state
aiActions.description: Re-fetch the linked issue/PR and update status + closed_by_pr if changed since the doc was last touched.

aiActions.name: Reclassify relationship
aiActions.description: Re-read the sibling issue and challenge the current relationship classification (same-root-cause / symmetric / adjacent / superseded / prior-art).

maintainer-stance.name: Maintainer Stance
maintainer-stance.description: Named, evolving positions held by stakeholders. Distinct from settled decision records — a stance has an owner, a timestamp, and may be rebutted or shift over time.
maintainer-stance.structuralContract: One-column card grid. Each card is one stakeholder's position with rationale and any rebuttal. Use when the section preserves an evolving debate — not a settled team decision.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: A stance is a named position in a live conversation. It has an owner, a timestamp, and may still move. Do not collapse stances into decisions.
inspect: Verify the stated_at timestamp links to the original comment or statement.
Check whether the stance has shifted since — update evolution accordingly.
update: When a stance is rebutted or softens, add to rebuttal/evolution rather than overwriting position.
avoid: Do not promote a stance to a decision before the team actually decides.
Do not erase a stance that was retracted — mark it retracted and preserve the history.

maintainer-stance.icon: <path opacity='.28' d='M4 6h12v10H4z'/><circle cx='8' cy='10' r='1.5'/><circle cx='14' cy='10' r='1.5'/><path d='M6 14h3M11 14h3' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' stroke-linecap='round'/>

aiActions.name: Check evolution
aiActions.description: Re-read the thread since stated_at; propose updating position, rebuttal, or evolution if the stance has shifted.

symptom-observation.name: Symptom Observation
symptom-observation.description: Observed behaviors with reproduction paths and witness attribution. Distinct from generic findings — the presence of repro steps and a named witness is the semantic contract.
symptom-observation.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card is one observed behavior with environment, reproduction steps, and a witness. Use when the section captures reproducible behaviors of a bug or feature — not generalized findings.
notFor: decisions or positions (use maintainer-stance or decision-record)

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: A symptom is a reproducible behavior. Without repro steps and a witness, it is not a symptom — it is a generic finding and belongs elsewhere.
inspect: Verify the repro steps actually reproduce — if the environment shifted, mark unconfirmed.
Check whether any other observer contradicted the symptom.
update: When a symptom is independently reproduced, keep the original witness and add the corroboration to notes.
avoid: Do not promote a generic finding to a symptom without a real repro path.

symptom-observation.icon: <circle cx='10' cy='10' r='6' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><path d='M10 6v4M10 12v.5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-linecap='round'/>

aiActions.name: Suggest environment variants
aiActions.description: Propose reproduction attempts on adjacent environments (other OS, terminal, framework version) to narrow or widen the symptom.

aiActions.name: Check for contradictions
aiActions.description: Scan the thread + orbit cards for observers who reported different behaviour; flag contradicted_by.

sections.title: Symptoms
sections.role: Hold reproducible observed behavior with environment, witness, and repro steps.
sections.rationale: Reproducible behaviours with attributable witnesses. Every card needs repro steps and a named observer — that is what separates a symptom from a generic finding.

sections.title: Findings
sections.role: Record synthesized facts learned during investigation that are not themselves repro steps.
sections.rationale: Synthesized claims that are not specific reproducible observations — e.g. 'the ANSI codes are idempotent' or 'the framework re-initializes on focus change'. If you find yourself writing a repro path, move the card to symptoms. Delete this section if every observation is repro-backed.

sections.title: Code anchors
sections.role: Pin the code locations, revisions, and mechanics that can plausibly explain or repair the issue.
sections.rationale: Revision-pinned pointers into the source. A reader or agent can jump straight to the file and line — no regrep required. The pinned revision makes drift visible on re-crystallize.

sections.title: Attempts
sections.role: Track probes, rejected fixes, shipped workarounds, and what each attempt proved.
sections.rationale: Every action tried against the problem and what it proved. Probes get marked superseded when a shipped attempt productionizes the same insight. Rejected attempts stay — they tell the next reader what not to try.

sections.title: Issue orbit
sections.role: Map sibling issues and PRs that share root cause, constraints, or prior resolution evidence.
sections.rationale: Graph of sibling issues and PRs with an explicit relationship to the focal one. If the relationship is weak (only topical overlap), drop the card — orbit is for structural relationships, not 'this also mentions mouse'.

sections.title: Stances
sections.role: Preserve stakeholder positions that constrain what fix is acceptable or what uncertainty remains.
sections.rationale: Named positions from stakeholders in the thread — maintainer, contributor, reporter. Stances are not decisions; they evolve. Preserve the original text so the next contributor doesn't re-litigate.

relationships.rationale: A deep-dive is not actionable until observed symptoms are tied to revision-pinned code mechanics.

evidence.description: A code anchor should identify the symptom card it explains, bounds, or makes inspectable.

relationships.rationale: Anchors become useful when an attempt probes, modifies, or rules out the mechanics they identify.

evidence.description: An attempt should name the anchor, code path, or touched path it is testing.

relationships.rationale: Attempt outcomes should be captured as findings before they become final recommendations or future work.
Related issues and PRs should explain whether the symptom is isolated, recurring, duplicated, or already partly fixed.
Maintainer and contributor positions should shape which repair attempts are acceptable, blocked, or worth avoiding.

stageSignals.when: symptom-observation has no repro-backed symptom cards
stageSignals.question: What observed behavior, witness, environment, and repro path make this issue tangible?

stageSignals.when: symptoms exist but code-anchor has no corresponding mechanics
stageSignals.question: Which symptom lacks a revision-pinned code anchor or mechanical explanation?

stageSignals.when: code anchors exist but attempt-log has no probes against them
stageSignals.question: Which code anchor still needs a probe, rejected attempt, or shipped workaround?

stageSignals.when: issue orbit or maintainer stance may have shifted since the last deep-dive pass
stageSignals.question: What changed in related issues, PRs, or stakeholder positions since this doc was last updated?

stageSignals.when: symptoms, anchors, attempts, issue orbit, and maintainer stances are populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Can a fresh contributor start engineering work from this doc without repeating the issue archaeology?

validOperations.label: Add symptom
validOperations.description: Add a repro-backed symptom card with environment, witness, and reproduction steps.

validOperations.label: Add code anchor
validOperations.description: Add a revision-pinned code anchor that explains, bounds, or makes a symptom inspectable.

validOperations.label: Add attempt
validOperations.description: Add a probe, rejected fix, or shipped workaround against a code anchor.

validOperations.label: Record finding
validOperations.description: Record the factual finding produced by an attempt before using it as handoff guidance.

validOperations.label: Refresh issue orbit
validOperations.description: Refresh related issues, PRs, and maintainer stances before treating the handoff as current.

proof-canonicality.name: Proof and Canonicality
proof-canonicality.title: Proof and Canonicality
proof-canonicality.subtitle: A starting shape for model, parser, protocol, or truth-claim work where assertions, findings, and proof strength matter.
proof-canonicality.scope: A domain where formal claims, staged proof, findings, and decisions need to be kept distinct but connected.
proof-canonicality.objectiveRole: Make a truth-bearing domain legible by separating model, assertion, proof, findings, decisions, and tooling.
proof-canonicality.templateObjective: Make a truth-bearing domain legible by separating the model, the assertion, the proof ladder, the findings, the decisions, and the tooling.
proof-canonicality.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can tell what is being claimed, what proof exists, where evidence is weak, what was learned, and which tools are used to evolve or verify the claim.

decision-record.name: Decision Record
decision-record.description: A compact set of decisions or operating choices that should be treated as authoritative ground truth.
decision-record.structuralContract: One-column card grid of page-status items, usually ground-truth or reference. Use when the section captures decisions, operating models, or declared truths rather than implementation work.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat decisions as authoritative ground truth or reference statements, not implementation work.
inspect: Check whether the decision is still current before marking it ground-truth.
update: Keep decision entries compact and authoritative.
avoid: Do not mix factual findings, verification rungs, or component status into decisions.

aiActions.name: Check if still current
aiActions.description: Read recent commits, tickets, and sessions for signals that a ground-truth decision has been implicitly overridden. Propose softening to reference or opening a challenge card.

formal-model.name: Formal Model
formal-model.description: A formal model surface spanning multiple primitives, invariants, or authorities in parallel.
formal-model.structuralContract: Two-column card grid of model-integrity items with optional definition, authority scope, and violations. Use when several model elements need to be compared or held together at once.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat the section as a parallel surface of model primitives, invariants, authorities, and integrity status.
inspect: Check the authoritative source and current violations before updating integrity status.
update: Keep model definitions and authority boundaries explicit.
avoid: Do not collapse broad model inventory into one claim.

investigation-findings.name: Investigation Findings
investigation-findings.description: A set of factual findings recovered from an investigation, review, or forensic pass.
investigation-findings.structuralContract: Two-column card grid of page-status items, usually ground-truth findings. Use when the section is a collection of observed findings rather than decisions or implementation tasks.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat findings as observed facts recovered from an investigation, distinct from decisions or planned work.
inspect: Check the underlying investigation evidence before adding or changing a finding.
update: Keep findings factual and separate from recommendations or decisions.
avoid: Do not turn findings into decisions, formal claims, or generic status items.

aiActions.name: Check still holding
aiActions.description: Re-verify the finding against current evidence. Propose status transitions (ground-truth / reference / deprecated) or flag as stale.

model-assertion.name: Model Assertion
model-assertion.description: A singular formal claim about the model, usually canonicality, authority, or one downstream truth path.
model-assertion.structuralContract: One-column card grid of model-integrity items. Use when the section is asserting one compact model truth rather than surveying many model elements in parallel.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat the section as one compact formal claim whose authority, primitives, invariants, and status must stay clear.
inspect: Check the governing authority and linked primitives before updating claim status.
update: Keep the assertion singular and avoid broad model inventory drift.
avoid: Do not use this for broad formal model inventories or verification checklists.

proof-ladder.name: Proof Ladder
proof-ladder.description: An ordered ladder of verification steps where each rung increases proof strength toward operational truth.
proof-ladder.structuralContract: Two-column card grid of probe-status items, typically named as levels or rungs. Use when the section expresses staged proof escalation rather than a flat set of checks.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat proof as staged escalation where each rung increases confidence toward operational truth.
inspect: Check whether evidence actually satisfies the rung before advancing status.
update: Preserve ladder ordering and make weaker or missing rungs explicit.
avoid: Do not flatten proof levels into unordered checks or decisions.

aiActions.name: Check monotonic invariant
aiActions.description: Confirm every rung below this one is ready. If not, flag the inversion — this rung cannot legitimately claim ready state.

aiProfiles.name: Proof State Brief
aiProfiles.description: Summarize what the ladder currently proves and where the proof boundary sits.

aiProfiles.name: Weakest Open Rung
aiProfiles.description: Identify the first unsatisfied rung and the missing evidence for closure.

aiProfiles.name: Next Stronger Proof
aiProfiles.description: Describe the next evidence shape that would strengthen the ladder without redesigning it.

status-snapshot.name: Status Snapshot
status-snapshot.description: A summary section that compresses the current document into top-level counts and distribution signals.
status-snapshot.structuralContract: Stats-first section with snapshot cards and no item grid requirement. Use for high-level totals and state distribution, not for individual entities.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat the section as document-wide compression, not as an item inventory.
inspect: Read the rest of the document before changing snapshot stats.
update: Recompute stats and callout language from the current section set and payload.
avoid: Do not add individual work items here.

status-snapshot.icon: <rect x='4' y='14' width='3' height='6' rx='1' opacity='.4'/><rect x='10.5' y='9' width='3' height='11' rx='1' opacity='.65'/><rect x='17' y='5' width='3' height='15' rx='1'/>

tooling-surface.name: Tooling Surface
tooling-surface.description: An operational tooling surface that collects the skills, scripts, workflows, and local instruments used to inspect, verify, or modify the domain without treating them as source material.
tooling-surface.structuralContract: Two-column card grid of tooling-status items. Each item should represent one operational instrument with clear purpose, invocation, and caveats. Use when the section is about how the domain is worked on rather than what the domain says.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat the section as the operational instrument layer used to inspect, verify, modify, or refresh the domain.
inspect: Check whether each tool is currently trusted, caveated, prototype, broken, or missing.
update: Keep purpose, when-to-use, invocation, and caveats explicit for each tool.
avoid: Do not treat tooling entries as primary source material or domain claims.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize proof posture and canonicality pressure without collapsing the evidence layers.
sections.rationale: This section keeps proof posture visible while leaving claims, evidence, findings, decisions, and tooling in their own accountable layers.

sections.title: Formal Model
sections.role: Define the primitives, invariants, authorities, and scope that make the domain truth-bearing.
sections.rationale: A truth-bearing domain needs explicit primitives, invariants, authorities, and scope before assertions can be evaluated.

sections.title: Model Assertion
sections.role: State the claims that the proof structure is trying to support or weaken.
sections.rationale: Assertions are kept separate from the model and proof so claim strength can change without rewriting the underlying domain.

sections.title: Proof Ladder
sections.role: Track staged evidence rungs that increase confidence in the assertions.
sections.rationale: Proof strength needs staged rungs so weak evidence, partial proof, and canonical proof do not collapse into one status.

sections.title: Investigation Findings
sections.role: Record observed facts learned from proof work without turning them directly into decisions.
sections.rationale: Findings capture what proof work established before those observations are promoted into decisions.

sections.title: Decision Record
sections.role: Preserve canonical choices justified by the model, proof, and findings.
sections.rationale: Decisions belong in their own section because canonical choices must be justified by the model, proof ladder, and findings.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose the scripts, skills, and workflows used to inspect, prove, or evolve the domain.
sections.rationale: Proof claims need operational tool paths so evidence can be rerun, challenged, or extended.

relationships.rationale: Assertions are only meaningful inside the model primitives, invariants, and authority scope they claim against.
A model assertion should not advance toward canonicality without staged proof strength.

evidence.description: A proof rung should identify the assertion, primitive, invariant, or authority it supports or challenges.

relationships.rationale: Proof work should produce factual findings before those findings become operating decisions.
Decisions should be justified by observed findings, not by unsupported assertions.
Proof rungs need an operational tool path so the evidence can be rerun or extended.

stageSignals.when: formal-model has no source-backed cards
stageSignals.question: What first model primitive, invariant, or authority makes this proof domain tangible?

stageSignals.when: model-assertion exists but proof-ladder has no corresponding proof rungs
stageSignals.question: Which assertion lacks proof support, or which proof rung is attached to the wrong claim?

stageSignals.when: proof-ladder has rungs but investigation-findings has no observed findings
stageSignals.question: What finding did the current proof rung actually establish?

stageSignals.when: model, assertion, proof, findings, decisions, and tooling are all populated and linked
stageSignals.question: What claim can responsibly become canonical, and what uncertainty remains?

validOperations.label: Add model primitive

validOperations.label: Add proof rung

validOperations.description: Record the factual finding produced by proof work before deciding what it means.

starter-tiny-experiment.name: Run Tiny Experiments
starter-tiny-experiment.title: Run Tiny Experiments
starter-tiny-experiment.subtitle: A small starting shape for work where personal, time-bounded experiments need an explicit hypothesis, window, and persist/pause/pivot closure.
starter-tiny-experiment.scope: A small set of running and recently-closed pacts, treated as the unit of learning rather than the unit of finishing.
starter-tiny-experiment.objectiveRole: Make time-bounded pacts operational by separating hypotheses, signals, attempts, and closure decisions.
starter-tiny-experiment.templateObjective: Make the experiments you are actually running visible — their hypotheses, their windows, and the closure decision waiting at the end of each.
starter-tiny-experiment.templateSuccessCondition: Every running pact has an end date and at least one trackable signal. Every closed pact has an outcome (persist / pause / pivot) and a one-or-two-sentence rationale.

attempt-log.name: Attempt Log
attempt-log.description: A record of actions taken against a problem and what each one proved. Distinct from findings (observations) and decisions (chosen directions).
attempt-log.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card is one attempt with an outcome. Status is attempt-log-status. Use when capturing tried fixes, probes, and shipped workarounds — not to record decisions or generic findings.
notFor: positions in an ongoing debate (use maintainer-stance)

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat each card as an action with a result. Every attempt must name what was tried and what it proved; shipped attempts must link their shipping site.
inspect: Verify each attempt has a concrete outcome — probes that revealed nothing are noise.
Check the shipped_in URL resolves and the referenced code or patch still exists.
update: When an attempt is superseded by a newer one, mark it superseded rather than deleting.
Preserve rejected attempts — they tell the next reader what not to try.
avoid: Do not collapse attempts into observations or decisions.
Do not mix generic notes into attempt cards — supporting context goes in notes[].

aiActions.name: Propose supersession
aiActions.description: If a newer attempt productionised the same insight, suggest marking this card superseded and linking the newer shipping site.

aiActions.name: Find shipping commit
aiActions.description: Search the referenced repos for the commit that productionised this attempt; fill shipped_in if missing.

status-snapshot.name: Status Snapshot
status-snapshot.description: A summary section that compresses the current document into top-level counts and distribution signals.
status-snapshot.structuralContract: Stats-first section with snapshot cards and no item grid requirement. Use for high-level totals and state distribution, not for individual entities.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat the section as document-wide compression, not as an item inventory.
inspect: Read the rest of the document before changing snapshot stats.
update: Recompute stats and callout language from the current section set and payload.
avoid: Do not add individual work items here.

tiny-experiment.name: Tiny Experiment
tiny-experiment.description: A small, time-bounded experiment with an explicit hypothesis and a closure decision of persist, pause, or pivot. The unit of pact-shaped learning.
tiny-experiment.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card is one experiment with a hypothesis, a window, the signals it watches, and on closure an outcome with rationale. Status is pact-status; outcome is pact-outcome and is only set when status is closed.
notFor: ongoing tasks without a hypothesis or window (use attempt-log)
cohort views across multiple experiments (use experiment-evidence-surface)
settled decisions with no live signal (use decision-record)

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Each card is a pact: a declarative hypothesis, a window with start and end, the signals being tracked, and a closure that picks one of three legitimate moves — persist, pause, or pivot. The closure carries the data; the running state is just the wait.
inspect: Check that every running card has a window end date — undated experiments quietly turn into permanent commitments.
Check that closed cards have an outcome and a one or two sentence rationale.
If a card has been running past its window without a closure, that is itself a signal — either close it or extend the window deliberately.
update: When the window closes, set status=closed, set outcome, and write the rationale before moving on. Skipping the rationale loses the data the experiment was trying to gather.
If pivoting, link nextPact to the follow-up experiment so the chain stays traceable.
Persisting does not mean unchanged — write down what is being kept and what (if anything) is being adjusted.
avoid: Do not use this for tasks that have no hypothesis. Tasks without a question being asked belong in attempt-log or operating-surface.
Do not collapse persist/pause/pivot into pass/fail — the three-way choice is the point.
Do not let cards drift past their window without a closure. The closure is what makes it an experiment.

aiActions.name: Check window elapsed
aiActions.description: If the window end has passed and status is still running, propose closure — surface the signals collected so far and ask the user for an outcome and rationale.

aiActions.name: Draft closure rationale
aiActions.description: Read the signals and any linked attempts, then propose a one or two sentence rationale for the chosen outcome. Author still confirms.

sections.title: Status Snapshot

sections.rationale: This section keeps current experiment pressure visible while pact hypotheses and attempts stay inspectable.

sections.role: Track each pact with hypothesis, window, signals, and closure state.
sections.rationale: Each experiment is a pact: hypothesis, time window, signals, and closure decision are the unit of learning.

sections.role: Record actions tried during or around the experiments and what they proved.
sections.rationale: Attempts preserve what was tried so pact outcomes are grounded in behavior rather than memory.

relationships.rationale: Experiment status should summarize actual running or recently closed pacts.
Pact closure should be informed by what was tried and what it proved.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize delivery pressure and current readiness without carrying the detailed evidence.
sections.rationale: This section keeps delivery readiness visible without mixing summary state into the design, alignment, verification, or tooling evidence.

sections.title: Design–Code–Spec Flow
sections.role: Hold the product surface as a joined design, code, spec, interaction, and ticket entity.
sections.rationale: The product surface needs one joined entity that can name design intent, implementation paths, specs, interactions, and tickets together.

sections.title: Design–Implementation Alignment
sections.role: Judge whether implementation targets satisfy the design and spec intent carried by the surface flow.
sections.rationale: The surface-flow cards need explicit implementation judgments so visual or code presence is not mistaken for delivery alignment.

sections.title: Verification Checkpoints
sections.role: Track the checks that prove the aligned implementation is ready enough to ship or continue.
sections.rationale: Alignment claims need concrete checks that can be rerun or extended before readiness is treated as real.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose the scripts, workflows, and local tools used to inspect, evolve, and verify the surface.
sections.rationale: Verification and future updates need visible tool paths so the surface can be inspected without rediscovering the workflow.

relationships.rationale: Status is only meaningful when it summarizes the actual surface flow being delivered.
Alignment rows should judge the design/code/spec entities named in the surface flow.

evidence.description: An alignment row should identify the surface-flow card, Figma node, or code path it judges.

relationships.rationale: Implementation alignment is not delivery readiness until checks exist for the aligned behavior.
Verification checkpoints need an operational tool path so a future session can rerun or extend them.
